title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for an urban wildlife survey. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Dana, The Hedgehog Mapper
Dana joined the urban wildlife survey after finding hedgehog footprints in the mud behind her block of flats one wet evening. [47]She began cutting small folded paper tunnels and filling them with harmless ink so residents could record paw prints overnight without disturbing animals.[/47] Her daily job as a postal worker helped her plan routes between gardens without wasting time after sunset. She expected people to be amused, but not committed. [48]The surprise was that several residents started leaving tiny gaps under fences after seeing proof that hedgehogs travelled between their gardens.[/48] Dana learned to explain the project without making people feel guilty about neat lawns. The hardest evenings were rainy ones, when the paper curled and the delicate prints blurred completely. Still, she loved checking tunnels at dawn before work. She says the survey made invisible night journeys visible, and that changed how neighbours talked about shared green spaces.
texts.B:
### Phil, The Birdsong Recorder
Phil recorded birdsong for the survey, using a borrowed directional microphone and an app he barely understood at first during practice. [49]His former career as a music producer helped him filter background traffic noise and identify when two recordings were actually the same bird singing from different trees.[/49] He enjoyed the cold early mornings, although buses and delivery vans ruined many attempts. [50]His biggest embarrassment was confidently labelling a ringtone from someone’s phone as an unusual warbler.[/50] After that, he checked every strange sound carefully with two experienced volunteers. Phil became fascinated by how much wildlife could be heard from supermarket car parks, railway bridges and school roofs. He also trained himself to stand still long enough for ordinary garden birds to resume singing around him. He says the work changed his ears; now a quiet street nearby no longer sounds empty, only layered with things he had ignored.
texts.C:
### Rosa, The Pond Watcher
Rosa monitored small overlooked ponds in parks and private back gardens, looking for frogs, beetles, newts and signs of pollution. [51]She was recruited by a neighbour who knew she had once studied chemistry and could test water samples accurately and safely.[/51] Rosa liked the careful weekly routine of filling tubes, comparing colours and noting smells in pencil. [52]The most upsetting discovery was a pond where detergent from a nearby drain had left dead insects floating along one edge.[/52] She reported it immediately to the council and helped the owner divert the dirty water. Children often joined her with damp notebooks, asking whether tadpoles had names. Rosa learned to balance scientific caution with their obvious excitement. She never falsely promised that every pond would recover, but she showed families how to add shade, stones and plants. For Rosa, the survey proved that even tiny water places deserve serious attention from adults.
texts.D:
### Kamal, The Night Walk Leader
Kamal led monthly night walks for residents who wanted to look patiently for bats and moths but felt uneasy going out after dark in parks. [53]He planned the route to avoid bright shopfronts, because artificial light made it harder to notice movement above the dark trees.[/53] His outdoor education background helped him set clear, simple safety rules without spoiling the quiet sense of adventure. [54]He was nervous the first time a participant panicked when a bat flew low over the group.[/54] Kamal calmly paused, explained the animal’s behaviour and waited until everyone nervously laughed. Later, after discussing it, he added a short introduction before each evening walk. [55]He keeps attendance numbers low so people can hear the small detector and ask questions without crowding each other on the path.[/55] [56]His favourite result was a council officer joining one walk and later supporting carefully darker lighting near the river path.[/56]

questions.question: used simple ink devices to collect evidence of animal movement overnight?
questions.explanation: Dana made paper tunnels with harmless ink so residents could record hedgehog paw prints.

questions.question: saw residents change their fences after receiving proof of animal routes?
questions.explanation: Dana says residents left gaps under fences after seeing proof that hedgehogs travelled between gardens.

questions.question: used sound-production experience to distinguish recordings in a noisy environment?
questions.explanation: Phil’s music production background helped him filter traffic noise and identify duplicate bird recordings.

questions.question: mistook a human-made sound for a bird call?
questions.explanation: Phil labelled a phone ringtone as an unusual warbler.

questions.question: was recruited because of knowledge useful for testing water?
questions.explanation: Rosa was recruited by a neighbour who knew she had studied chemistry.

questions.question: found evidence that pollution from a drain had damaged a pond?
questions.explanation: Rosa discovered detergent from a drain had left dead insects in a pond.

questions.question: planned walks to reduce the effect of artificial light on observations?
questions.explanation: Kamal avoided bright shopfronts because artificial light made movement harder to notice.

questions.question: had to calm someone frightened by an animal during an activity?
questions.explanation: Kamal calmed a participant who panicked when a bat flew low.

questions.question: limits group size so participants can listen and ask questions properly?
questions.explanation: Kamal keeps attendance low so people can hear the detector and ask questions.

questions.question: gained practical support from an official who attended an activity?
questions.explanation: A council officer joined Kamal’s walk and later supported darker lighting near the river.
